title: Der alte Sultan
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 48. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The forty-eighth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 48), a beast-fable of loyalty and cunning. Old Sultan, a toothless dog his master means to shoot, is saved by a scheme of his friend the wolf — a staged kidnapping and rescue of the master’s child; but when the wolf demands sheep in payment, Sultan stays loyal and betrays him, and the boar-and-wolf duel collapses into farce when a three-legged cat’s raised tail is mistaken for a sabre. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, todt, Thier, Hausthüre, gescheidt, gieng, ward, mußte, Muthes and verrathen.

sentences.t: Es hatte ein Bauer einen treuen Hund, der Sultan hieß, der war alt geworden und hatte alle Zähne verloren, so daß er nichts mehr fest packen konnte.
sentences.l: There was a farmer who had a faithful dog called Sultan, who had grown old and had lost all his teeth, so that he could no longer grip anything firmly.
sentences.i: A farmer once had a faithful dog named Sultan, who had grown old and lost all his teeth, so that he could no longer get a firm hold on anything.
sentences.n: The tale opens with the dummy subject “es” filling the front slot (“Es hatte ein Bauer …” = ‘a farmer had …’). “so daß” keeps the 1857 ß spelling (modern dass).

sentences.t: Zu einer Zeit stand der Bauer mit seiner Frau vor der Hausthüre und sprach „den alten Sultan schieß ich morgen todt, der ist zu nichts mehr nütze.“
sentences.l: At one time the farmer stood with his wife before the house-door and said, “old Sultan I will shoot dead tomorrow, he is good for nothing any more.”
sentences.i: One day the farmer was standing with his wife in front of the house door and said, “I’m going to shoot old Sultan dead tomorrow; he’s no use for anything now.”
sentences.n: “todt” and “Hausthüre” keep the 1857 -th spelling (modern tot, Haustüre). “zu nichts nütze” is a fixed phrase, ‘good for nothing.’ Fronting the object (“den alten Sultan schieß ich”) throws the emphasis onto the dog.

sentences.t: Die Frau, die Mitleid mit dem treuen Thiere hatte, antwortete „da er uns so lange Jahr gedient hat und ehrlich bei uns gehalten, so könnten wir ihm wohl das Gnadenbrot geben.“
sentences.l: The wife, who had pity on the faithful animal, answered, “since he has served us so many a year and kept honestly by us, we might well give him the bread of charity.”
sentences.i: The wife, who felt pity for the faithful creature, answered, “since he has served us so many years and stayed loyally by us, we might well let him have his bread of grace.”
sentences.n: “das Gnadenbrot geben” — the Gnadenbrot is the ‘bread of grace,’ the keep given to a worn-out servant or animal past its work; the whole tale turns on it. “Thiere” keeps the 1857 -th (modern Tiere).

sentences.t: „Ei was,“ sagte der Mann, „du bist nicht recht gescheidt: er hat keinen Zahn mehr im Maul, und kein Dieb fürchtet sich vor ihm, er kann jetzt abgehen.
sentences.l: “Ei what,” said the man, “you are not quite sensible: he has not a tooth left in his mouth, and no thief is afraid of him, he can go now.
sentences.i: “Bah,” said the man, “you’re not being sensible: he hasn’t a tooth left in his mouth, and no thief is afraid of him; he can be off now.
sentences.n: “gescheidt” is the 1857 spelling of gescheit (‘clever, sensible’). “sich fürchten vor” = to be afraid of. “Ei was” is a dismissive exclamation, roughly ‘bah, nonsense.’

sentences.t: Hat er uns gedient, so hat er sein gutes Fressen dafür gekriegt.“
sentences.l: Has he served us, then he has got his good feed for it.”
sentences.i: If he has served us, why, he has had his good feed in return.”
sentences.n: A conditional without wenn: fronting the verb (“Hat er uns gedient …”) means ‘if he has served us.’ “Fressen” is the word for an animal’s food, as opposed to human Essen. “gekriegt” is colloquial for bekommen (‘got’).

sentences.t: Der arme Hund, der nicht weit davon in der Sonne ausgestreckt lag, hatte alles mit angehört und war traurig daß morgen sein letzter Tag sein sollte.
sentences.l: The poor dog, who lay stretched out in the sun not far from it, had listened to it all and was sad that tomorrow was to be his last day.
sentences.i: The poor dog, who lay stretched out in the sun not far off, had heard the whole of it and was grieved that tomorrow was to be his last day.
sentences.n: “mit angehört” — the mit reinforces ‘listened in on all of it.’ “sein sollte” uses sollen for what is appointed or destined to happen.

sentences.t: Er hatte einen guten Freund, das war der Wolf, zu dem schlich er Abends hinaus in den Wald und klagte über das Schicksal, das ihm bevorstände.
sentences.l: He had a good friend, that was the wolf, to whom he slipped out in the evening into the forest and complained about the fate that lay before him.
sentences.i: He had a good friend, the wolf, to whom he slipped out one evening into the forest, and he lamented the fate that lay ahead of him.
sentences.n: “bevorstände” is a subjunctive (reported thought) of bevorstehen, ‘to be impending’ — a form Grimm reaches for whenever reporting what a character feared or thought.

sentences.t: „Höre, Gevatter,“ sagte der Wolf, „sei gutes Muthes, ich will dir aus deiner Noth helfen.
sentences.l: “Hear, gossip,” said the wolf, “be of good courage, I will help you out of your need.
sentences.i: “Listen, old friend,” said the wolf, “be of good cheer; I’ll help you out of your trouble.
sentences.n: “Gevatter” literally means ‘godfather,’ but here it is a familiar term of address between the dog and wolf, roughly ‘neighbour, old friend.’ “sei gutes Muthes” is a set genitive phrase, ‘be of good cheer’ (Muthes = 1857 spelling of Mutes).

sentences.t: Ich habe etwas ausgedacht.
sentences.l: I have thought something out.
sentences.i: I’ve thought of something.

sentences.t: Morgen in aller Frühe geht dein Herr mit seiner Frau ins Heu, und sie nehmen ihr kleines Kind mit, weil niemand im Hause zurückbleibt.
sentences.l: Tomorrow in the very early morning your master goes with his wife into the hay, and they take their little child along, because no one stays behind in the house.
sentences.i: Tomorrow at the crack of dawn your master goes out haymaking with his wife, and they take their little child along, since no one is left behind in the house.
sentences.n: “ins Heu gehen” — literally ‘to go into the hay,’ i.e. to go haymaking. “nehmen … mit” is the separable verb mitnehmen (‘to take along’), its prefix mit flung to the end of the clause — you’ll see this constantly.

sentences.t: Sie pflegen das Kind während der Arbeit hinter die Hecke in den Schatten zu legen: lege dich daneben, gleich als wolltest du es bewachen.
sentences.l: They are wont to lay the child during the work behind the hedge in the shade: lay yourself beside it, just as if you wanted to guard it.
sentences.i: During the work they usually lay the child behind the hedge in the shade; lie down beside it, as though you meant to guard it.
sentences.n: “pflegen … zu” = ‘to be in the habit of, be wont to.’ “gleich als wolltest du” — ‘just as if you wanted to’; the subjunctive wolltest sets up a pretended intention, which is the heart of the ruse.

sentences.t: Ich will dann aus dem Walde herauskommen und das Kind rauben: du mußt mir eifrig nachspringen, als wolltest du mir es wieder abjagen.
sentences.l: I will then come out of the forest and rob the child: you must spring eagerly after me, as if you wanted to chase it away from me again.
sentences.i: Then I’ll come out of the forest and snatch the child; you must dash eagerly after me, as though you meant to win it back from me.
sentences.n: This lays out the staged rescue: the wolf will pretend to seize the child so the dog can pretend to save it. “abjagen” = to take something from someone by chase; with the double object mir es, ‘to hunt it away from me.’

sentences.t: Ich lasse es fallen, und du bringst es den Eltern wieder zurück, die glauben dann du hättest es gerettet und sind viel zu dankbar als daß sie dir ein Leid anthun sollten: im Gegentheil, du kommst in völlige Gnade, und sie werden es dir an nichts mehr fehlen lassen.“
sentences.l: I let it fall, and you bring it back to the parents again; they then believe you had rescued it, and are far too grateful for them to do you any harm: on the contrary, you come into complete favour, and they will let you lack for nothing more.”
sentences.i: I’ll let it drop, and you carry it back to the parents; then they’ll think you rescued it, and be far too thankful to do you any harm — on the contrary, you’ll come into full favour, and they’ll see that you want for nothing.”
sentences.n: The ruse in full: the fake theft and fake rescue. “du hättest es gerettet” is subjunctive II of reported belief (‘that you had rescued it’). “zu dankbar als daß …” is the ‘too … to’ construction. “ein Leid anthun” (1857 for antun) = ‘to do harm’; “es an nichts fehlen lassen” = to let one lack for nothing.

sentences.t: Der Anschlag gefiel dem Hund, und wie er ausgedacht war, so ward er auch ausgeführt.
sentences.l: The scheme pleased the dog, and as it had been thought out, so it was also carried out.
sentences.i: The plan pleased the dog, and just as it had been devised, so it was carried out.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘was, became’); modern German uses wurde, and you will meet ward throughout Grimm. Note the “wie … so” frame, ‘as … so.’

sentences.t: Der Vater schrie als er den Wolf mit seinem Kinde durchs Feld laufen sah, als es aber der alte Sultan zurückbrachte, da war er froh, streichelte ihn und sagte „dir soll kein Härchen gekrümmt werden, du sollst das Gnadenbrot essen, so lange du lebst.“
sentences.l: The father cried out when he saw the wolf running through the field with his child, but when old Sultan brought it back, then he was glad, stroked him and said, “to you not a little hair shall be bent, you shall eat the bread of charity as long as you live.”
sentences.i: The father cried out when he saw the wolf running off across the field with his child; but when old Sultan brought it back, he was overjoyed, stroked him and said, “not a hair of yours shall be harmed; you shall eat the bread of grace as long as you live.”
sentences.n: “dir soll kein Härchen gekrümmt werden” — literally ‘to you not a little hair shall be bent,’ a set idiom for ‘no harm shall come to you’; Härchen is the diminutive of Haar, and the dative dir marks whose hair it is.

sentences.t: Zu seiner Frau aber sprach er „geh gleich heim und koche dem alten Sultan einen Weckbrei, den braucht er nicht zu beißen, und bring das Kopfkissen aus meinem Bette, das schenk ich ihm zu seinem Lager.“
sentences.l: But to his wife he said, “go home at once and cook old Sultan a bread-pap, which he need not bite, and bring the pillow out of my bed, that I give him for his resting-place.”
sentences.i: And to his wife he said, “go straight home and cook old Sultan a soft bread-porridge that he won’t have to bite, and bring the pillow from my bed — I’ll give him that for his couch.”
sentences.n: “Weckbrei” is a pap of soaked roll or bread, soft because the toothless dog cannot chew. “Lager” here is the dog’s bed or lair, not a warehouse.

sentences.t: Von nun an hatte es der alte Sultan so gut, als er sichs nur wünschen konnte.
sentences.l: From now on old Sultan had it as good as he could only wish for himself.
sentences.i: From then on old Sultan was as well off as he could ever have wished.
sentences.n: “sichs” = sich + es contracted (‘it for himself’); such contractions are common in the 1857 text.

sentences.t: Bald hernach besuchte ihn der Wolf, und freute sich daß alles so wohl gelungen war.
sentences.l: Soon after, the wolf visited him, and rejoiced that everything had succeeded so well.
sentences.i: Soon afterwards the wolf visited him and was delighted that everything had turned out so well.
sentences.n: “hernach” is an older word for ‘afterwards.’ “gelingen” (‘to succeed, turn out well’) takes sein as its auxiliary: gelungen war.

sentences.t: „Aber Gevatter,“ sagte er, „du wirst doch ein Auge zudrücken, wenn ich bei Gelegenheit deinem Herrn ein fettes Schaf weghole.
sentences.l: “But gossip,” said he, “you will surely press one eye shut when I on occasion fetch away a fat sheep from your master.
sentences.i: “But old friend,” said he, “surely you’ll turn a blind eye when I now and then carry off a fat sheep from your master.
sentences.n: “ein Auge zudrücken” — literally ‘to press one eye shut,’ the exact idiom for ‘to turn a blind eye.’ Here the wolf names his price for the rescue.

sentences.t: Es wird einem heutzutage schwer sich durchzuschlagen.“
sentences.l: It grows hard for one nowadays to make one’s way through.”
sentences.i: It’s hard for a body to get by these days.”
sentences.n: “einem” is the impersonal dative, ‘for one, for a person.’ “sich durchschlagen” = to fight one’s way through, to scrape by.

sentences.t: „Darauf rechne nicht,“ antwortete der Hund, „meinem Herrn bleibe ich treu, das darf ich nicht zugeben.“
sentences.l: “Do not reckon on that,” answered the dog, “to my master I stay faithful, that I may not allow.”
sentences.i: “Don’t count on that,” answered the dog, “I stay true to my master; I cannot permit it.”
sentences.n: “darauf rechne nicht” — rechnen auf, ‘to count on.’ Fronting “meinem Herrn” (dative, with treu bleiben ‘to stay loyal to’) stresses the master. “zugeben” here means ‘to allow, permit.’

sentences.t: Der Wolf meinte das wäre nicht im Ernste gesprochen, kam in der Nacht herangeschlichen und wollte sich das Schaf holen.
sentences.l: The wolf supposed that was not spoken in earnest, came creeping up in the night and wanted to fetch himself the sheep.
sentences.i: The wolf thought this was not meant in earnest, came sneaking up in the night, and meant to help himself to the sheep.
sentences.n: “das wäre nicht im Ernste gesprochen” — the subjunctive wäre marks reported thought (‘supposed that it was not …’). “im Ernste” keeps the old dative -e ending.

sentences.t: Aber der Bauer, dem der treue Sultan das Vorhaben des Wolfes verrathen hatte, paßte ihm auf und kämmte ihm mit dem Dreschflegel garstig die Haare.
sentences.l: But the farmer, to whom the faithful Sultan had betrayed the wolf’s intention, waited for him and combed his hair nastily with the threshing-flail.
sentences.i: But the farmer, whom faithful Sultan had warned of the wolf’s design, lay in wait for him and gave his hide a nasty combing with the flail.
sentences.n: “kämmte ihm … garstig die Haare” — literally ‘combed his hair nastily,’ an ironic idiom for a sound beating. “verrathen” is the 1857 spelling of verraten (here ‘disclosed, gave away’). “paßte … auf” = aufpassen, to watch or lie in wait for.

sentences.t: Der Wolf mußte ausreißen, schrie aber dem Hund zu „wart, du schlechter Geselle, dafür sollst du büßen.“
sentences.l: The wolf had to run away, but cried out to the dog, “wait, you bad fellow, for that you shall atone.”
sentences.i: The wolf had to bolt, but shouted back to the dog, “just wait, you wretched fellow — you’ll pay for this.”
sentences.n: “mußte” keeps the 1857 ß (modern musste). “ausreißen” = to bolt, run off. “dafür sollst du büßen” — büßen, ‘to atone, pay for’; a stock threat.

sentences.t: Am andern Morgen schickte der Wolf das Schwein, und ließ den Hund hinaus in den Wald fordern, da wollten sie ihre Sache ausmachen.
sentences.l: The next morning the wolf sent the pig, and had the dog summoned out into the forest, where they meant to settle their affair.
sentences.i: The next morning the wolf sent the boar, and had the dog called out into the forest, there to settle their quarrel.
sentences.n: “das Schwein” here is the wild boar (called “das wilde Schwein” below). “ließ … fordern” — lassen + infinitive, ‘had him summoned/challenged.’ “ausmachen” = to settle, sort out a matter.

sentences.t: Der alte Sultan konnte keinen Beistand finden als eine Katze, die nur drei Beine hatte, und als sie zusammen hinaus giengen, humpelte die arme Katze daher und streckte zugleich vor Schmerz den Schwanz in die Höhe.
sentences.l: Old Sultan could find no support but a cat that had only three legs, and when they went out together, the poor cat hobbled along and at the same time, from pain, stretched her tail up into the air.
sentences.i: Old Sultan could find no ally but a cat with only three legs, and as they went out together, the poor cat came limping along, and from the pain held her tail stuck up in the air.
sentences.n: “Beistand” = support, a second in a duel. “giengen” keeps the 1857 spelling of gingen. This sets up the running joke: the upright tail (Schwanz) will be taken for a weapon.

sentences.t: Der Wolf und sein Beistand waren schon an Ort und Stelle, als sie aber ihren Gegner daher kommen sahen, meinten sie er führte einen Säbel mit sich, weil sie den aufgerichteten Schwanz der Katze dafür ansahen.
sentences.l: The wolf and his second were already on the spot, but when they saw their opponent coming along, they supposed he carried a sabre with him, because they took the cat’s upright tail for one.
sentences.i: The wolf and his second were already on the spot; but when they saw their opponent approaching, they thought he was carrying a sabre, for they took the cat’s upright tail to be one.
sentences.n: The joke pays off: the raised tail is mistaken for a Säbel (sabre). “an Ort und Stelle” is a fixed phrase, ‘on the very spot.’ “ansehen … für” (here dafür) = ‘to take for.’ “führte” is subjunctive of reported belief.

sentences.t: Und wenn das arme Thier so auf drei Beinen hüpfte, dachten sie nicht anders als es höbe jedesmal einen Stein auf, wollte damit auf sie werfen.
sentences.l: And whenever the poor animal hopped so on three legs, they thought nothing else than that it lifted up a stone each time, meant to throw it at them with it.
sentences.i: And each time the poor creature hopped along on its three legs, they were sure it was picking up a stone to hurl at them.
sentences.n: “dachten … nicht anders als” = ‘thought nothing other than,’ i.e. were convinced. “höbe” is the subjunctive II of heben (‘to lift’), marking their imagined, unreal fear. “Beinen” is the old dative plural of Bein.

sentences.t: Da ward ihnen beiden angst: das wilde Schwein verkroch sich ins Laub, und der Wolf sprang auf einen Baum.
sentences.l: Then both of them grew afraid: the wild boar crept away into the foliage, and the wolf sprang up onto a tree.
sentences.i: At that both took fright: the wild boar buried himself in the leaves, and the wolf sprang up a tree.
sentences.n: “ward ihnen beiden angst” — an impersonal idiom, ‘fear came over them both’ (dative ihnen beiden). “verkroch sich” — sich verkriechen, ‘to creep away and hide.’

sentences.t: Der Hund und die Katze, als sie heran kamen, wunderten sich daß sich niemand sehen ließ.
sentences.l: The dog and the cat, when they came up, wondered that no one let himself be seen.
sentences.i: The dog and the cat, when they came up, wondered that no one was to be seen.
sentences.n: “sich sehen lassen” — literally ‘to let oneself be seen,’ i.e. to show oneself, to appear.

sentences.t: Das wilde Schwein aber hatte sich im Laub nicht ganz verstecken können, sondern die Ohren ragten noch heraus.
sentences.l: But the wild boar had not been able to hide himself quite in the foliage, but rather his ears still jutted out.
sentences.i: But the wild boar had not managed to hide himself completely in the leaves; his ears still stuck out.
sentences.n: “sondern” (‘but rather’) follows a negative and corrects it: not fully hidden — rather, the ears sticking out.

sentences.t: Während die Katze sich bedächtig umschaute, zwinste das Schwein mit den Ohren: die Katze welche meinte es regte sich da eine Maus, sprang darauf zu und biß herzhaft hinein.
sentences.l: While the cat looked about her warily, the boar twitched with his ears: the cat, who thought a mouse was stirring there, sprang at it and bit heartily into it.
sentences.i: While the cat looked warily about, the boar twitched his ears; the cat, thinking a mouse was stirring there, pounced and bit lustily into them.
sentences.n: “zwinste” (from zwinsen, ‘to blink, twitch’) is a rare dialectal form. “es regte sich da eine Maus” uses impersonal es with the real subject eine Maus, ‘a mouse was stirring there.’

sentences.t: Da erhob sich das Schwein mit großem Geschrei, lief fort und rief „dort auf dem Baum da sitzt der Schuldige.“
sentences.l: Then the boar rose up with a great outcry, ran off and cried, “there on the tree, there sits the guilty one.”
sentences.i: At that the boar leapt up with a great squealing, ran off, and cried, “up there on the tree sits the guilty one!”
sentences.n: “Schuldige” is a substantivized adjective, ‘the guilty one’ (from schuldig, ‘guilty’).

sentences.t: Der Hund und die Katze schauten hinauf und erblickten den Wolf, der schämte sich daß er sich so furchtsam gezeigt hatte und nahm von dem Hund den Frieden an.
sentences.l: The dog and the cat looked up and caught sight of the wolf, who was ashamed that he had shown himself so fearful and accepted peace from the dog.
sentences.i: The dog and the cat looked up and spied the wolf, who was ashamed of having shown himself so cowardly, and made peace with the dog.
sentences.n: “schämte sich daß …” — sich schämen, ‘to be ashamed.’ “nahm … den Frieden an” — annehmen, ‘to accept’; the comic reconciliation closes the tale.
